text:
This review focuses on recent advances in the field of gas-phase transition metal clusters as a quantitative probe into surface chemistry and heterogeneous catalysis at the atomic level. This area of research has evolved considerably over the past few decades largely driven by the development of size-selective experimental methods such as infrared (IR) action spectroscopy enabling direct structural information of isolated clusters and their complexes in the gas phase without any complications arising from the strong interactions with substrates or solvents. Recent advancements in high-intensity, widely tunable light sources like infrared free-electron lasers (IR-FELs) have allowed for an in-depth investigation into the vibrational modes of molecular ligands and hence provided quantitative information concerning the sites of binding, mechanisms of bond activation and the subsequent reaction steps.

A broad spectrum of clusters containing small molecules, involving more specifically the binding and activation of H2, CO, N2, O2 as well as triatomic and larger organic molecules, is the subject of a detailed analysis on a large number of cluster species. It is illustrated with numerous examples how cluster size, charge and structure determine the reactivity leading frequently to non-monotonic trends often expressed as "every atom counts." Finally, the possible role of IR-induced reactions (such as dehydrogenation or CO oxidation reactions in clusters) with a potential role for clusters in studies of elementary catalytic steps are illustrated, putting to work the cluster as a nanoscale catalytic laboratory. In summary, the work illustrates at quantitative level how mass resolved vibrational spectroscopy of gas phase clusters can serve as a quantitative validation/starting point for novel or unexpected results from state of the art quantum chemical calculations, a premise being central to the design and optimisation of novel nanocatalysts.
